speaker: interviewer
text: It is February 25, 1997, and we're interviewing a rescue and aid-giver, Renee Scott. Ms. Scott, please tell me, why-- why was it that you got involved with the underground and helping the Jews during the Second World War?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I told you. I started with Simone very evasively, because I had to go to Belgium several times, and she got a passport. And I started to go like that, and she asked me once if I would bring documents. And I did, without knowing they were documents. But I brought papers in Brussels. And then, I did that several times. And then, one time, I got back to Paris, and she said, I have to tell you the truth. And she told me what she was doing since, already, a long time. I think, maybe, she started in '38. I don't know. She was very young.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, she said, well, here's what you've been doing for me. Have you any remorse? Or, do you think I should not have asked you? I said, no. I started. I'm continuing. That's it. Do you have any advice to give to people today about helping other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: By helping other people? Well, I think you could be a little more careful than I was, if you have responsibilities like I did. I had responsibility of a daughter that didn't have her father with her. And I think, maybe, I should have thought, well, my daughter needs a mother with her, too. That, I didn't do. And I think people should think. I didn't think enough. I think they should think what they're doing a little better than I did. I don't regret it, though. I don't regret it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm glad I did it. And do you have any last words you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any last words you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I said, through all my ordeal, that I can say that I always trusted God, and I knew he would bring me back. And I always prayed for all my friends I had left, especially all my family. I knew they were looking after me, although they weren't with me. And I knew I would come back. I didn't know how, but I knew I'd come back. And I still thank God every morning and every night for bringing me back. And I am happy for what I did do to help people. And I will always try to help people. I'm very happy that I was able to help so many Jewish people. Because I did. Through Simone, I helped them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Scott, on behalf of everyone who's seen this, I want to say, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't hear you.

speaker: interviewer
text: On behalf of everyone who has seen this or will see this, I want to say, thank you for everything you've done.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. I must-- I want to say, thank you?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm saying thank you to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. That's what I said. Thank you very much. Well, I did it. I did it with all my heart. And I'm-- I'm always ready to help people. I don't say I'd go back in a camp, though. But I'm always happy to help. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know my mother, at a certain time, had reproached to me that I should have told them something, because they suffered a long 3 and 1/2 years of silence without knowing where I was. If I had told them, well, you know, I'm doing this and this, then maybe, they would have said-- 'cause our minister tried to find me in Belgium. He couldn't find me. Everywhere he went, they said, she's gone without leaving an address. That meant you were dead. So of course, my mom, my parents, my family suffered a lot by my silence.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] Thank you, Mrs. Scott.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name's Renee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Renee.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Renee Mickey. [LAUGHS] Don't mention it. I'm happy if I can help. If this is any help, I don't know. So you're going to send me a little tape? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is the photo of?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents' wedding in Calais, France, in 1897.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my mother gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's in the photo, other than your mother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents, my aunts, and my uncles-- aunts and uncles, and both grandparents-- grandmother, grandpa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you name any of them specifically, starting with the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't see that [? way ?]. I know there is my-- my Aunt Edith, my Aunt [? Cissy, ?] [? Cecille. ?] Anymore ladies there? [? Amelia, ?] [? Amelia. ?] There is my grandma, my grandfather. On the other side, there's my father and my mother. And, on the other side, my uncles. There's my Uncle [? Emil, ?] my Uncle George, my Uncle Paul, and one more uncle, I think. One more uncle-- [? Rene. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a troupe from Paris. It was called, in Tunis, North Africa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the dancers-- part of the dancers and actors in the troupe, you know. And we were waiting for a couscous dinner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, which one are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the white thing on-- black dress and a white collar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister's in there as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I can't see where she is. I think she's climbing in the tree, there. She's in the tree somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I get this? Oh, my goodness. When I went-- when we took it, we all had-- they all gave us a picture. The boys, you know, from the troupe, took it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In approximately what year was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Written in back. 1930, I think. I think so. You saw it yesterday. Remember? [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] There were some better ones than that, though. You didn't take the better ones. That one's worn out. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This is a picture taken in Nancy, at the opera, where we danced for five years, my sister and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is that photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of me, Renee Scott. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In approximately what year was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1931. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] This is a picture of my sister, May, and myself in Lille. This is in Lille, at the opera. But in between times, we had the opera and we had the-- the lighter Sebastopol Theater. And that was taken at the Sebastopol, always in Lille, north of France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately when was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1931, about the same date.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which dancer is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am on the left. [LAUGHS] My sister, May, on the right. This is a picture taken in 1940, beginning of the war, with Simone and I at the Belgium Chamber of Commerce [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a few months before we were arrested.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one is Simone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Simone is on the left. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Lieutenant parachutist, sorry. That's Lillian, one of my very best friends. She was lieutenant parachutist for the International Underground. And she survived a shot that she received by the Gestapo in her back-- came out under her heart. She survived that because she was saved by farmers, right in the fields, where she dropped. But she killed the German, and the farmers took care of her and got her better. And I met her again in the camp in Ravensbruck.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Lillian's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freter-- F-R-E-T-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know approximately when this photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's after we returned, probably in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- how did you get a hold of the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave them to me. I was her best friend. I lived with her. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This cross was given to me, presented to me by the Prince Regent of-- of Belgium, Prince Charles of Belgium. And the-- it's mounted on a pin that the Countess of Croix from Sweden gave me before I took the ship to come home. And my-- my badge is mounted with six stars, and it is represents the Legion of Honor. [PAUSES FOR 41 SECONDS] This plaque was offered to me at B'nai B'rith up in Chestnut Hill, at a big dinner that Mr. Larkin and his friends offered to me in recognition of what I had done during the war for the Jewish people. And I'm very happy to have this in my hands. [PAUSES FOR 30 SECONDS] These pictures were taken at-- not city hall but the other one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Government Center?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, yeah, but where Mr. Dukakis was.

speaker: interviewer
text: State House?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they asked me if I would come and tell some of my experiences about the Holocaust, which I did. And I was very happy to even be able to talk to everybody. And Mr. Dukakis was very nice, also his wife Kitty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this gentleman in the picture with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is Cardinal Law with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately when was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in 1990, I think. I remember his name. This was taken 1990, the same day as I was speaking up for Governor Dukakis. And here, we have the Speaker of the House-- I don't remember his name-- and Governor Dukakis, myself, telling them my experiences. And there's Kitty and Cardinal Law. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is a picture taken after the dinner at B'nai B'rith in 1981, the dinner Mr. Larkin and his friends had offered us. And on this picture, there is my daughter on the left, my granddaughter Patty, Patricia, my granddaughter Carole, Patty's husband, James, and myself, in the middle. [PAUSES FOR 23 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Paris. I don't know. There's no date on the photo, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my daughter, taken in Paris. And I presume it was in early-- in the early '40s, '40, '44, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the context of the picture? Was this from a movie [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. That, yeah-- I don't think she was in the movies there. But it was one of her pictures in the movies. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] That must have been-- this is a photo of my grandson, who is now a lieutenant colonel in the Marines. And that's when he graduated from Suffolk University here, I think. So, it's quite a number of years ago. But now he lives in California, and he is now the father of a big boy, five months old-- almost five months old-- by the name of Curt Lieber. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He's a wonderful grandson, did a good job. This is a story that my wonderful little great-granddaughter wrote about me. She was-- she goes to Latin Academy School, and they asked the children to write a story on their family-- somebody of their family. And she chose her grandmother-- her great-grandmother, who is me. And she wrote a story about me, about all of my experiences in the camp and, I think, my return. And she won a computer of $3,000 from-- I don't know who-- the one that makes the spaghettis and all those things. They're the ones who gave the computer. And I think she really made a very good story of it. She had a lot of research to find-- you know, to write what she did. And she got pictures together. I never knew anything about it. [LAUGHS] She surprised me with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did she write it?

speaker: interviewee
text: A year and a half ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, '95.
